speaker: narrator
text: He'd been three days out of Ashford when he smelled the smoke -- the wrong kind, not hearth-fire or forge-smoke but the flat, mineral bite of stone that had changed its mind about being stone.

speaker: narrator
text: He'd been heading nowhere in particular, the way a man does when the war is over and the shape of peacetime hasn't settled yet. A hedge-knight's work: a missing horse here, a disputed boundary there. Small things. Useful things.

speaker: narrator
text: Things that didn't ask him to be more than he was.

speaker: narrator
text: Then the smoke.

speaker: narrator
text: Thornwick still smoked when Caelin rode in at first light.

speaker: narrator
text: Ash muffled the cobbles, the horse's hooves swallowed by the soft texture and depth of the soot-packed street. Melted stone and hot glass mingling with the acrid bite of charred timber.

speaker: narrator
text: Houses hadn't burned so much as slumped: wooden beams turned translucent, doorframes sagging into glossy curves, window mullions weeping clear droplets that froze mid-fall.

speaker: narrator
text: Even the lane stones had softened and set again, their surfaces ridged like cooled lava.

speaker: narrator
text: He'd seen dragonfire before -- in the war, villages reduced to char and ash in minutes. But this was different. This fire hadn't consumed. It had transformed.

speaker: narrator
text: A cry -- thin, desperate, trapped -- cut through the silence.

speaker: narrator
text: He ran.

speaker: narrator
text: At the end of the south lane, a cottage had folded in on itself like a paper lantern crushed by an invisible fist.

speaker: caelin
text: "Hold on!"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin called, dropping to his knees in the debris.

speaker: caelin
text: "Where are you?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Here,"

speaker: narrator
text: a small voice gasped, barely audible through the rubble.

speaker: caelin
text: "It's hot -- please -- "

speaker: narrator
text: He found her beneath a collapsed roof beam: maybe eight years old, freckled face streaked with soot, pinned by timber that still shimmered with residual heat. Her eyes were wide with terror, tracking the glow in the wood above her.

speaker: caelin
text: "You're going to be all right,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said, setting his palm against the scorching beam.

speaker: narrator
text: He called the flame the way he always had -- shaping heat to eat along the grain, to reduce wood to ash and set her free. But the magic answered differently this time. The beam didn't char or crumble. It softened.

speaker: narrator
text: Fibers gave way beneath his touch, going glassy and viscous, sloughing under his hand in a slow, honey-thick peel. He drew the beam aside in a translucent sheet; the instant it left contact with his skin, it hardened with a sharp crack and shattered.

speaker: narrator
text: The girl stared up at him with wonder and fear in equal measure.

speaker: narrator
text: He stared at his own hand.

speaker: caelin
text: "Fire shouldn't do this,"

speaker: narrator
text: he murmured, more to himself than to her.

speaker: narrator
text: He gathered the child up in his arms, feeling how she trembled against him.

speaker: caelin
text: "What's your name?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Mira."

speaker: caelin
text: "I'm Caelin. Close your eyes now. The air's bad out here."

speaker: narrator
text: Outside, where the square's wind provided some relief from the smoke, she curled into his travel cloak like it was the only safe thing left in the world. Her lips trembled as she tried to form words.

speaker: caelin
text: "Mama said to hide,"

speaker: narrator
text: she whispered finally.

speaker: caelin
text: "But the red lady saw me anyway. She spoke to me."

speaker: caelin
text: "The dragon?"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin asked, though something in his chest already knew the answer.

speaker: narrator
text: Mira nodded, her small fingers clutching his cloak tighter.

speaker: caelin
text: "She said she was looking for the ember-born. She said she was sorry but she had to know."

speaker: narrator
text: The child's voice dropped to barely audible.

speaker: caelin
text: "Then she sang something. A rhyme. Over and over while the fire came. I... I remember it."

speaker: narrator
text: Before he could respond, Elder Gareth emerged from the smoke -- one cheek blistered raw, his beard singed away on that side, but his eyes still steady with the kind of strength that comes from bearing witness to catastrophe.

speaker: caelin
text: "You saved one,"

speaker: narrator
text: the old man rasped, his voice thick with smoke and grief.

speaker: caelin
text: "The gods bless you for it."

speaker: mira
text: "She wasn't hunting,"

speaker: narrator
text: Mira whispered against Caelin's shoulder.

speaker: caelin
text: "She kept saying she was sorry. That she had to find someone."

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth nodded grimly.

speaker: mira
text: "Dragons kill clean when they feed -- quick, then gone. This was deliberate. Methodical. She melted the shrine down to its foundation stones but left the granary untouched. Destroyed the inn but spared the healer's cottage."

speaker: narrator
text: His eyes met Caelin's.

speaker: caelin
text: "She was testing. Calling out in the old tongue, waiting for something to answer."

speaker: narrator
text: Something prickled along Caelin's forearms, a readiness sharpening his awareness. He'd spent years studying fragments of Draconic in dusty libraries, and his dreams had carried sounds and syllables that weren't his own.

speaker: narrator
text: Now the anticipation felt physical, like standing on the edge of a precipice, waiting for the moment gravity takes hold.

speaker: narrator
text: Mira tugged at his sleeve with surprising insistence.

speaker: mira
text: "The rhyme. Do you want to hear it?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Please,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said, though the answer was already pressing against his ribs like a held breath.

speaker: narrator
text: The child swallowed hard and recited in a thin, earnest voice:

speaker: narrator
text: Nine the arts and nine the lights,

speaker: narrator
text: Nine the souls to set them right;

speaker: narrator
text: Keep the chain and guard the frame --

speaker: narrator
text: Wake not the Tenth that has no name.

speaker: narrator
text: The words settled into him like keys finding locks, fitting into spaces he hadn't known were waiting to be filled.

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth pointed beyond the mill with a hand that trembled slightly.

speaker: mira
text: "Her trail ends past the hedgerow. The ground there is..."

speaker: narrator
text: He struggled for words.

speaker: caelin
text: "The earth has turned to glass. Black glass, like obsidian. There's a crater, and at the center..."

speaker: narrator
text: He shook his head.

speaker: mira
text: "You should see it yourself."

speaker: caelin
text: "I'll go,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said, because any other response felt like turning away from something that had been calling him for years.

speaker: narrator
text: He didn't look back at Mira. He was afraid if he did he would stay, and staying felt like the wrong kind of cowardice.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin left Mira in the elder's care and followed the path of scorched hedge and melted stone. The destruction formed a clear line -- deliberate, purposeful -- leading away from the village center toward the fields beyond.

speaker: narrator
text: Mira's rhyme clung to him like smoke. Each word seemed to settle behind his ribs, waiting for something that hadn't yet awoken.

speaker: narrator
text: Where the hedgerow ended, the earth had transformed entirely.

speaker: narrator
text: A shallow crater yawned before him, perhaps fifty feet across. Its floor and walls were black glass -- pure obsidian that caught the morning light and threw it back in dark rainbows.

speaker: narrator
text: Crystalline spires jutted up from the rim like the fingers of a buried giant reaching for sky. Steam rose in lazy spirals, carrying the sharp mineral scent of transformed stone.

speaker: narrator
text: At the center lay the dragon.

speaker: narrator
text: She was vast beyond mortal scale, easily the size of Gareth's barn. Her hide had dulled from coal-red to ash-crimson, fissured along her flanks where internal heat still glowed like forge embers struggling against the dark.

speaker: narrator
text: Each breath came as a failing rasp, wet and rattling. Her wings lay crumpled and torn, one bent at an angle that made Caelin wince. But her eyes -- old gold, ancient as mountains -- still held purpose.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin slid down the crater's inner slope. The glass sang beneath his boots, a low crystalline hum that resonated in his chest.

speaker: narrator
text: The dragon's eye tracked his descent and fixed on him.

speaker: narrator
text: Her voice arrived not as sound but as meaning pressing directly into his mind -- old Draconic rendered crystalline and clear, as if a fog he hadn't known was there had suddenly lifted.

speaker: mira
text: "At last,"

speaker: narrator
text: she said, and relief colored the words.

speaker: caelin
text: "The bloodline answers."

speaker: narrator
text: He went to one knee, instinct deeper than thought.

speaker: mira
text: "Why did you do this? All those people -- "

speaker: caelin
text: "I had no choice."

speaker: narrator
text: came the answer, heavy with genuine regret.

speaker: mira
text: "I am dying. I felt it three days ago -- the rot in my heart, the fire going cold. And I carry something that cannot die with me. Something that must pass to one who can bear it."

speaker: narrator
text: Her eye closed briefly.

speaker: caelin
text: "I searched the nearby villages. Called out in the old tongue. Used fire to test who might answer, who might have the gift to work flame differently than others."

speaker: mira
text: "You killed them to test them?"

speaker: narrator
text: Horror and anger warred in his chest.

speaker: caelin
text: "I tested the fire,"

speaker: narrator
text: she corrected, and there was no defensiveness in it -- only weary fact.

speaker: mira
text: "Most would have burned. Only one with dragon-blood could have shaped what I wrought. Only the ember-born could have turned transformation back to wood, glass back to timber."

speaker: narrator
text: Her eye opened and fixed on him again.

speaker: caelin
text: "You saved the child. You proved what you are."

speaker: narrator
text: The truth of it settled cold in his stomach. She'd been searching for him. And people had died in that search.

speaker: mira
text: "What are you?"

speaker: narrator
text: he asked, voice rough.

speaker: caelin
text: "Vharisax, last guardian of the First Concord. And my time ends."

speaker: narrator
text: She shifted slightly, and the movement cost her.

speaker: mira
text: "But the compact must continue. The Nine must be found. The Seal must remain closed."

speaker: narrator
text: "The rhyme," Caelin said. "The child remembered it. Nine the arts -- "

speaker: vharisax
text: "Nine that weave the world,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vharisax said. Images flooded his mind -- dragons and dwarves forging side by side, a name carved above a gate in a script older than any living tongue, nine pedestals blazing with power, and beneath them something vast and chained, pressing against its bonds. The weight of it left Caelin gasping.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin's breath had gone shallow. He didn't know when that had happened.

speaker: vharisax
text: "The Nine maintain balance,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vharisax continued.

speaker: mira
text: "Each school of magic bound into physical form, each requiring a bearer to claim it, to become it. When all nine are claimed and the bearers stand together, the Seal remains closed. But if the Nine are scattered, lost, or broken..."

speaker: narrator
text: Her breathing hitched.

speaker: vharisax
text: "The Tenth stirs. And if it wakes fully, the world drowns in entropy made conscious."

speaker: mira
text: "Why me?"

speaker: narrator
text: The question came out smaller than he intended.

speaker: vharisax
text: "You carry the old blood. Faint, yes -- diluted through generations -- but present. You can bond with what I give you. You can survive it."

speaker: narrator
text: Her foreclaw trembled as she lifted it.

speaker: mira
text: "And you have no choice. I am dying now. If I do not pass this on, it dies with me. The compact breaks. The chain weakens."

speaker: narrator
text: Nestled against talon and scale lay a plate of obsidian-red, roughly the length of his thumb, hexagonal edges still carrying forge-heat. It pulsed with inner light -- ember-motes drifting in its translucent depths.

speaker: narrator
text: Not a shed scale. An offering.

speaker: vharisax
text: "This is the key,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vharisax said.

speaker: mira
text: "Take it. Bind yourself. Follow the path I will leave you. Find the First Sanctum and claim what waits there -- the Dragon's Ember, first of the Nine, forged for Evocation itself."

speaker: narrator
text: Her eye held his.

speaker: vharisax
text: "And then find the others who must bear the rest. You cannot do this alone."

speaker: narrator
text: His hand hovered above the scale, every instinct screaming that this was a threshold he couldn't uncross.

speaker: mira
text: "What will it do to me?"

speaker: vharisax
text: "It will mark you. Visibly. Permanently."

speaker: narrator
text: No false comfort.

speaker: mira
text: "It will guide you toward what must be found. It will punish deception and reward truth. It will hurt, especially at first."

speaker: narrator
text: Her tone softened slightly.

speaker: vharisax
text: "And it will make you exactly what you already are -- only more so. The magic you've always carried will awaken fully. You will understand the old tongue. You will see the paths others cannot."

speaker: mira
text: "And if I refuse?"

speaker: vharisax
text: "Then I die carrying this burden. The compact weakens. The Seal cracks a little more. And in a generation, perhaps two, something that should never breathe air will taste sky."

speaker: narrator
text: She held his gaze.

speaker: mira
text: "I do not offer you glory, ember-born. I offer you necessity."

speaker: narrator
text: The weight of her certainty pressed against him. The crater, the transformed village, the child's terrified eyes -- all of it leading to this moment.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin closed his fingers around the scale.

speaker: narrator
text: Heat surged up his arm -- hot glass seeking its shape, reality rewriting itself cell by cell. For a moment the world went white, then cleared, he looked down at his right forearm.

speaker: narrator
text: A thumb-length, hexagonal plate lay flush with his skin just below the elbow -- dark as translucent obsidian, smooth as glass, with ember-motes drifting lazily in its depths like captured stars.

speaker: narrator
text: The edges were perfect, geometric, inlaid so seamlessly with his flesh that he couldn't tell where the scale ended and his skin began.

speaker: narrator
text: From its six edges, hair-fine black filaments spread like delicate root systems, threading beneath his skin in branching patterns. They ran toward his elbow, toward his wrist, mapping themselves along nerve pathways with dark precision.

speaker: narrator
text: He could feel them settling, finding their places, becoming part of his body's geography.

speaker: narrator
text: He flexed his fingers. The embedded plate flexed with him, the filaments shifting beneath his skin like living tattoo.

speaker: vharisax
text: "There,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vharisax said, and satisfaction colored her fading voice.

speaker: mira
text: "The bond is struck. You are marked. You are chosen."

speaker: vharisax
text: "It will answer you,"

speaker: narrator
text: she continued, each word coming slower.

speaker: mira
text: "And it will correct you. When you speak falsehood, it will sting. When you hesitate before necessity, it will push. When you attempt to flee your purpose..."

speaker: narrator
text: A thread of dark humor.

speaker: vharisax
text: "It will drag you back to the path."

speaker: mira
text: "I understand,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said.

speaker: narrator
text: The embedded plate snapped white-hot -- a needle of searing light that lanced through every nerve in his arm. He gasped, biting back a cry, and the glow subsided to steady ember.

speaker: vharisax
text: "Truth,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vharisax said, approval warming the word.

speaker: mira
text: "Already you learn. Never lie to the scale, ember-born. It knows you better than you know yourself."

speaker: narrator
text: He cradled his arm, feeling the residual heat pulse beneath his skin.

speaker: vharisax
text: "What do I do now?"

speaker: mira
text: "Follow the flame-path I will leave. It leads to the Emberpeaks, to the First Sanctum. There you will find the Dragon's Ember -- the first of the Nine Relics, the anchor of Evocation itself. Claim it. Bond with it fully."

speaker: narrator
text: Her breathing grew more labored.

speaker: vharisax
text: "Then seek the others. The bearers are out there, scattered, most unaware of what they're meant to become. You must find them. Gather them. Before the Seal breaks entirely."

speaker: mira
text: "And the Tenth?"

speaker: narrator
text: he asked.

speaker: vharisax
text: "What's truly buried there?"

speaker: mira
text: "Old,"

speaker: narrator
text: she whispered.

speaker: vharisax
text: "A bargain made in the world's first age. Power that should never have been. The Nine were forged to keep it sleeping."

speaker: narrator
text: Her voice dropped to bare thread.

speaker: mira
text: "If you hear it calling -- and you will, if you draw too close -- do not answer. Do not try to wake it. Not yet. Perhaps not ever."

speaker: narrator
text: Her chest rose once more, held, and fell for the final time. The glow along her fissured flanks dimmed -- and then, instead of darkness, it brightened.

speaker: narrator
text: Not the orange of living fire. Something older. Deeper.

speaker: narrator
text: The light moved through her scales the way flame moves through paper held to a candle -- catching at the edges first, the great plates along her spine curling inward as they ignited, translucent and amber before dissolving to nothing.

speaker: narrator
text: The transformation swept forward from her tail to her throat in a slow, inevitable tide.

speaker: narrator
text: Scale by scale, ridge by ridge, the body that had carried a thousand years of memory began to come apart -- not violently, not with heat or sound, but with the quiet authority of something that had decided it was finished.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin couldn't move. Couldn't look away.

speaker: narrator
text: Vharisax burned like a letter burning -- edges first, then the body of the thing, each piece glowing brilliant before it released. Her wings went last, the great membranes thinning to lace, to light, to nothing. Where she had lain, the crater floor held only the ghost of her outline in glass.

speaker: narrator
text: And then the embers came for him.

speaker: narrator
text: They rose in a slow column -- ten thousand motes of ash and amber light, drifting upward as if gravity had briefly lost interest in them. They turned in the air above the crater, unhurried, and Caelin understood with bone-deep certainty that they were not random. They were choosing.

speaker: narrator
text: The column tilted. Descended.

speaker: narrator
text: They settled on the scale.

speaker: narrator
text: One by one, and then in streams, and then all at once -- every mote of what had been Vharisax finding its way to the obsidian plate in his forearm, sinking into the translucent surface and becoming part of the ember-motes already drifting there.

speaker: narrator
text: The scale flared so bright he had to turn his face away. Heat moved through the filaments like a second bonding -- not painful this time, but immense. The weight of it. The age of it.

speaker: narrator
text: A thousand years of flight and memory and purpose flowing into six inches of hexagonal obsidian set in a man's arm.

speaker: narrator
text: When the last ember settled, the crater was empty.

speaker: narrator
text: The scale dimmed to its steady coal-glow. The filaments cooled. Caelin stood in the glass-smooth hollow where a dragon had died, and breathed, and tried to understand what he was carrying now.

speaker: narrator
text: From the crater's rim, the flame-path stretched away toward distant mountains -- fine as a thread of silk, bright as a road paved with light. It hadn't been there a moment ago. Or perhaps it had always been there, and something in him had finally learned to see it.

speaker: narrator
text: Footsteps crunched on glass behind him.

speaker: vharisax
text: "Well,"

speaker: narrator
text: a voice observed, dry as desert wind.

speaker: mira
text: "That looks profoundly uncomfortable."

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin spun, hand instinctively moving to the sword at his hip.

speaker: narrator
text: A woman leaned against the glass ridge, arms crossed, assessing him with the calm focus of a predator deciding if he was a threat or opportunity. Twin daggers rested at her hips, their hilts worn smooth with use.

speaker: narrator
text: Her travel cloak was torn and stained with ash, and one eye was green while the other shone gold, catching the light like a cat's.

speaker: narrator
text: Her expression suggested she found the entire situation darkly amusing.

speaker: vharisax
text: "Virella Sunshadow,"

speaker: narrator
text: she said, not moving from her perch.

speaker: mira
text: "Though people who haven't tried to murder me yet generally call me Vex."

speaker: caelin
text: "How long have you been there?"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin asked, still catching his breath from the bonding.

speaker: mira
text: "Long enough."

speaker: narrator
text: She pushed off from the stone and slid down the glass slope with practiced ease, boots finding purchase where he would have slipped.

speaker: caelin
text: "Long enough to watch a dragon die passing something to a stranger. Long enough to see that very permanent addition to your arm."

speaker: narrator
text: Her mismatched gaze dropped to the embedded scale, studying it with professional interest.

speaker: mira
text: "Interesting accessory. Not exactly subtle."

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin instinctively tried to cover it with his other hand, then stopped. What was the point? The scale was flush with his skin, inlaid like jewelry he could never remove.

speaker: caelin
text: "You shouldn't be here. This is -- "

speaker: mira
text: "Dangerous? Obviously."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex stopped a few feet away, close enough to talk comfortably, far enough to react if he proved hostile.

speaker: caelin
text: "But I've been tracking something dangerous for months now, so the ambiance feels appropriate."

speaker: mira
text: "Tracking what?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Answers."

speaker: narrator
text: She pulled a water-stained journal from inside her cloak and held it up briefly before tucking it away again.

speaker: mira
text: "My mother was a scholar. Obsessed with old alliances, ancient pacts, something she called 'fire-kept covenants.' She died six months ago in the Emberpeaks, and all I have left are her research notes and more questions than sense."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex gestured at the dragon's crystalline corpse.

speaker: caelin
text: "Her last journal entry mentioned rumors of a dragon searching for something. That was three weeks before she disappeared. So when I heard about a dragon burning villages in a very specific pattern -- not raiding, not feeding, but searching -- I followed the trail."

speaker: narrator
text: Her eyes met his.

speaker: mira
text: "And here I find not treasure, but you. With that."

speaker: narrator
text: A nod at his arm.

speaker: narrator
text: The scale pulsed warm against his skin, but didn't sting. Whatever else Vex might be hiding, she was being honest about her motivations.

speaker: caelin
text: "Your mother was looking for the same thing the dragon gave me,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said slowly.

speaker: mira
text: "Was she?"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex's tone sharpened with interest.

speaker: caelin
text: "And what exactly did that dying dragon give you, besides permanent body modification?"

speaker: narrator
text: He hesitated. The scale warmed in warning -- truth or pain, choose.

speaker: mira
text: "A burden. A purpose. A path to follow."

speaker: caelin
text: "How poetically vague."

speaker: narrator
text: But her eyes tracked to something behind him, and her expression shifted.

speaker: mira
text: "What are you staring at?"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin glanced back at the flame-thread stretching toward the mountains, then returned his attention to her.

speaker: caelin
text: "You can't see it?"

speaker: mira
text: "See what?"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex followed his gaze, squinting at empty air above the crater's rim.

speaker: caelin
text: "All I'm getting is heat shimmer and the worst headache I've had in months. Which, given my lifestyle, is saying something."

speaker: mira
text: "There's a path. Fire, but not fire. Leading into the Emberpeaks."

speaker: narrator
text: He flexed his marked arm, feeling the scale pulse in time with the distant thread.

speaker: caelin
text: "The dragon said only those marked could see it."

speaker: mira
text: "Of course she did."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex rubbed her temples.

speaker: caelin
text: "Let me guess -- this invisible magic road leads straight into the mountains where my mother vanished. Where the last three caravans disappeared without a trace. Where absolutely nothing good has been heard from in half a year."

speaker: mira
text: "Probably."

speaker: caelin
text: "Perfect."

speaker: narrator
text: She let her hands drop and fixed him with an evaluating look.

speaker: mira
text: "New arrangement, then. You need someone watching your back while you follow your glowing destiny path. I need someone who can actually see where we're going and potentially lead me to what my mother died seeking."

speaker: caelin
text: "We?"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin raised an eyebrow.

speaker: vex
text: "You walk in front,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex said with the practicality of someone who'd survived on the margins,

speaker: caelin
text: "drawing every monster's attention with that beacon in your arm. I follow behind, keeping us both alive with my actually useful skills like not getting eaten. Meanwhile, we both get closer to our answers."

speaker: vex
text: "That's your pitch? I'm bait?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Mutually beneficial bait."

speaker: narrator
text: Her mouth quirked.

speaker: vex
text: "Look, you're going into those mountains whether I come or not -- I can see it in your face. That scale's already made the choice for you. And I'm going regardless of whether you want company, because my mother's last camp was somewhere in those peaks and I'm not leaving until I know what happened to her."

speaker: narrator
text: She crossed her arms.

speaker: caelin
text: "So we can stumble into whatever's waiting separately and probably both die, or we can watch each other's backs and maybe one of us survives long enough to accomplish something. Your choice, ember-boy."

speaker: narrator
text: The nickname irritated him, but the logic was sound. And the scale wasn't stinging -- she meant what she said.

speaker: caelin
text: "You said your mother called them 'fire-kept covenants,'"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said.

speaker: vex
text: "Did her notes mention anything about Nine? Or about something sealed?"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex's expression sharpened.

speaker: caelin
text: "The Ninefold Concord. She had that phrase written everywhere in her later journals -- underlined, circled, with question marks. She thought it was some kind of alliance between dragons and... someone else. Dwarves, maybe. From before the kingdoms."

speaker: narrator
text: Her mismatched eyes narrowed.

speaker: vex
text: "Why?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Because the dragon spoke of Nine relics. Nine bearers. And something called the Tenth that has to stay sleeping."

speaker: narrator
text: The words felt too big, too important for the smoking crater and morning light.

speaker: vex
text: "Your mother was looking for the same thing I've just been bound to find."

speaker: narrator
text: For a long moment, Vex was silent, her expression cycling through several emotions before settling on grim determination.

speaker: caelin
text: "Then I'm definitely coming with you. If the thing that killed my mother is connected to whatever's in your arm, I want to be there when we find it."

speaker: caelin
text: "It might not have been a thing that killed her,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said quietly.

speaker: vex
text: "The mountains are dangerous even without ancient conspiracies."

speaker: caelin
text: "Maybe."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex's hand drifted to one of her daggers, a habitual gesture that spoke of comfort rather than threat.

speaker: vex
text: "But she was too good to die to something ordinary. Too careful. If something in those peaks took her, I need to know what. And I need to know if it's still there."

speaker: narrator
text: The scale pulsed steady and warm. No sting. Truth spoken plainly.

speaker: caelin
text: "We leave as soon as I can gather supplies from what's left of the village,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said.

speaker: vex
text: "The path leads east toward the Emberpeaks. The dragon said there's a sanctum there -- the First Sanctum, she called it. That's where we're going."

speaker: caelin
text: "We."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex's mouth twitched into something almost like a smile.

speaker: vex
text: "Look at that. Partnership established through mutual desperation and probable doom. My favorite kind."

speaker: caelin
text: "I should warn you,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin added, looking down at the scale embedded in his arm, watching the ember-motes drift in its translucent depths.

speaker: vex
text: "This thing has rules. It punishes lies. Forces truth. I'm not entirely in control of what I might say anymore."

speaker: vex
text: "Good,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex said, surprising him.

speaker: caelin
text: "Lies get people killed. I'd rather travel with someone who can't lie than someone I have to constantly second-guess."

speaker: narrator
text: She glanced at the dragon's body, her expression softening slightly.

speaker: vex
text: "Besides, after watching what just happened -- whatever that was -- I think we're past the point where normal rules apply."

speaker: narrator
text: They stood in the crater's heart, the morning sun climbing higher, burning away the last of the smoke. The flame-path stretched toward distant peaks, visible only to Caelin's marked sight. The scale pulsed its steady rhythm against his skin.

speaker: vex
text: "We should go,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex said.

speaker: caelin
text: "Whatever was drawn by the dragon's fire might still be nearby. And I'd rather not explain to the villagers why I was watching from the shadows while their savior got magically branded."

speaker: vex
text: "Fair point."

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin took one last look at Vharisax -- already transforming into monument, into legend, into the glass-frozen history of this place.

speaker: caelin
text: "Thank you,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said quietly, not entirely sure if he meant it for the burden or the purpose or simply for answering the question his entire life had been circling.

speaker: narrator
text: The dragon didn't answer. She'd said everything she needed to.

speaker: narrator
text: They climbed the crater's slope together, glass singing its low crystalline note beneath their boots. At the rim, Caelin paused, looking toward Thornwick's smoke and then toward the Emberpeaks' distant blue silhouette.

speaker: narrator
text: Behind them, the dragon lay in her crater of transformed earth.

speaker: narrator
text: Ahead, the flame-path beckoned with patient, inexorable pull.

speaker: narrator
text: And on his arm, the scale kept its steady count of heartbeats, marking time until destiny could no longer be delayed.

speaker: narrator
text: They returned to Thornwick as the sun climbed toward noon, walking through streets that would never be the same.

speaker: narrator
text: The transformation was everywhere -- glass-smooth walls that had been wood, crystalline puddles where water had boiled and frozen in the same instant, cobblestones fused into organic curves that caught light like gems.

speaker: narrator
text: People stared as they passed. Not at Vex -- she drew glances, certainly, with her mismatched eyes and road-worn bearing -- but at Caelin. At his arm.

speaker: narrator
text: He'd rolled up his sleeve without thinking, too hot from the climb out of the crater.

speaker: narrator
text: Now the embedded scale was visible to anyone who looked: that thumb-length hex of dark obsidian inlaid flush with his skin, the hair-fine filaments threading away from its edges like black veins beneath the surface.

speaker: narrator
text: The ember-motes drifted lazily in the scale's depths, visible even in daylight.

speaker: vex
text: "They're afraid,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex murmured beside him.

speaker: caelin
text: "I saved a child,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said, but even to his own ears it sounded hollow.

speaker: vex
text: "You did. And then you went to meet a dragon and came back changed."

speaker: narrator
text: She kept her voice low, conversational.

speaker: caelin
text: "People fear what they don't understand. And that -- "

speaker: narrator
text: a subtle gesture at his arm

speaker: vex
text: " -- they definitely don't understand."

speaker: narrator
text: The scale pulsed warm. No sting. She was right, as usual.

speaker: narrator
text: They found Elder Gareth in the village square, directing a handful of survivors as they loaded a wagon with salvaged supplies. He looked up as they approached, his gaze going immediately to Caelin's exposed forearm.

speaker: narrator
text: The old man's eyes widened, then narrowed with something that might have been recognition.

speaker: gareth
text: "So,"

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth said quietly, straightening with effort.

speaker: vex
text: "She marked you."

speaker: caelin
text: "You know what this is?"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin asked, surprise and relief mixing in equal measure.

speaker: vex
text: "I'm old enough to remember stories my grandmother told. Stories her grandmother told her."

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth stepped closer, studying the scale without touching.

speaker: caelin
text: "The dragon-marked. The ember-born. The old songs spoke of them -- those who carried fire in their blood and could be chosen as bearers for something greater."

speaker: narrator
text: His voice dropped.

speaker: vex
text: "I didn't think I'd live to see it proven true."

speaker: caelin
text: "The dragon -- Vharisax -- she said I have to find something. Nine somethings. Relics, she called them. And gather others who can bear them."

speaker: narrator
text: The words tumbled out faster than intended.

speaker: vex
text: "She said if I don't, something sealed beneath the mountains will wake. Something that shouldn't."

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth was quiet for a long moment, his weathered face unreadable. Then he nodded slowly.

speaker: caelin
text: "Then you'll go. You don't have a choice, do you?"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin looked down at the scale.

speaker: vex
text: "No. I really don't."

speaker: gareth
text: "The mark won't let you,"

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth said with certainty.

speaker: vex
text: "It'll pull you toward what needs doing. Push you when you hesitate. Drag you if you try to run."

speaker: narrator
text: He met Caelin's eyes.

speaker: gareth
text: "My grandmother said the dragon-marked were blessed and cursed in equal measure. The blessing: purpose absolute. The curse: no peace until that purpose is fulfilled."

speaker: vex
text: "That's about right,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex muttered.

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth turned his attention to her, assessing.

speaker: gareth
text: "And you are?"

speaker: vex
text: "Someone following him because he's following something I need to find."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex's honesty was almost aggressive.

speaker: gareth
text: "My mother died in those mountains looking for whatever he's bound to seek. I'm going to find out why."

speaker: narrator
text: The elder nodded as if this made perfect sense.

speaker: vex
text: "Then you'll watch his back while he watches the path. Good. He'll need someone practical."

speaker: narrator
text: He turned back to Caelin.

speaker: gareth
text: "When do you leave?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Today,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said, though he hadn't consciously decided. The scale warmed in agreement.

speaker: gareth
text: "The path leads to the Emberpeaks. The sooner we follow it, the sooner..."

speaker: narrator
text: He trailed off, unsure how to finish.

speaker: gareth
text: "The sooner you can rest,"

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth finished for him.

speaker: caelin
text: "If you ever can."

speaker: narrator
text: He clasped Caelin's shoulder with surprising strength.

speaker: gareth
text: "You saved my granddaughter. That's a debt Thornwick won't forget. If you need supplies, take them. If you need silver, we have a little. If you need a blessing -- "

speaker: narrator
text: his voice roughened

speaker: caelin
text: " -- you have mine. Go with purpose. Return with answers."

speaker: caelin
text: "I'll try,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin managed.

speaker: gareth
text: "Don't try. Do."

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth's grip tightened.

speaker: caelin
text: "The dragon chose you for a reason. Trust that, when everything else fails."

speaker: narrator
text: A small voice called out:

speaker: gareth
text: "Caelin!"

speaker: narrator
text: Mira broke away from a group of children and ran toward them, her soot-stained dress flapping. She skidded to a stop in front of him, eyes wide and bright.

speaker: caelin
text: "You're leaving,"

speaker: narrator
text: she said. Not a question.

speaker: gareth
text: "I have to."

speaker: caelin
text: "Because of what the red lady gave you."

speaker: narrator
text: Her gaze dropped to his arm, to the scale embedded there. She reached out one small hand, hesitated, then gently touched the obsidian surface with one fingertip.

speaker: narrator
text: The scale pulsed beneath her touch -- a single bright flare of light that made the ember-motes swirl faster. Mira gasped but didn't pull away.

speaker: gareth
text: "It's warm,"

speaker: narrator
text: she whispered.

speaker: caelin
text: "Like a heartbeat."

speaker: caelin
text: "It is, in a way,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said, his throat tight.

speaker: narrator
text: Mira pulled her hand back and looked up at him with an intensity that seemed too old for her age.

speaker: gareth
text: "The rhyme. The one the dragon sang. You're going to find what it talks about, aren't you? The Nine?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Yes."

speaker: gareth
text: "Will you come back?"

speaker: narrator
text: The question hit harder than expected. The scale stayed steady -- it would let him answer honestly either way.

speaker: caelin
text: "I don't know,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said.

speaker: gareth
text: "The path is long. What I'm looking for is dangerous. But if I can..."

speaker: narrator
text: He stopped, tried again.

speaker: caelin
text: "I'll try."

speaker: narrator
text: The scale stung -- sharp and bright. He winced.

speaker: narrator
text: Mira's eyes widened.

speaker: gareth
text: "It hurt you. Why did it hurt you?"

speaker: caelin
text: "Because I wasn't being truthful enough."

speaker: narrator
text: He knelt so they were eye-level.

speaker: gareth
text: "Let me try again. I don't know if I'll come back. What I'm doing might take years. It might take me to places I can't return from. But -- "

speaker: narrator
text: and this he knew was true, felt it bone-deep

speaker: caelin
text: " -- if I succeed, if I find what I'm meant to find, then places like Thornwick will be safer. Dragons won't come searching. Villages won't burn. Children won't lose their mothers."

speaker: narrator
text: The scale stayed warm. No sting. Truth accepted.

speaker: narrator
text: Mira nodded solemnly, then surprised him by wrapping her arms around his neck in a fierce hug.

speaker: gareth
text: "Then you have to succeed,"

speaker: narrator
text: she whispered against his shoulder.

speaker: caelin
text: "You have to."

speaker: gareth
text: "I will,"

speaker: narrator
text: he said, and hoped it wasn't a lie.

speaker: narrator
text: When she pulled back, she was crying, but her chin was set with determination.

speaker: caelin
text: "I'll remember the rhyme. Every word. And when you come back, I'll say it to you again so you know I kept it safe."

speaker: gareth
text: "I'd like that."

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth gently called Mira back, and she went reluctantly, looking over her shoulder until she disappeared into the group of children being loaded onto the wagon.

speaker: gareth
text: "They're evacuating to Millbrook,"

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth said.

speaker: caelin
text: "The village council there owes us a debt from the grain shortages five years back. We'll rebuild. Someday."

speaker: narrator
text: He looked around at the glass-transformed ruins.

speaker: gareth
text: "Or we'll build something new. Either way, we'll survive."

speaker: caelin
text: "I'm sorry,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said.

speaker: gareth
text: "If I'd come sooner -- "

speaker: caelin
text: "You came when you could. You saved who you could."

speaker: narrator
text: The elder's voice was firm.

speaker: gareth
text: "That's all anyone can ask. The rest is on the dragon, and she's answered for it."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex touched Caelin's arm -- the unmarked one.

speaker: caelin
text: "We should go. Afternoon's burning, and I'd rather be in the foothills before dark. Less chance of running into whatever might be drawn by the dragon's death."

speaker: narrator
text: She was right. The scale pulsed agreement.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin looked around at Thornwick one last time -- at the survivors, the transformed buildings, the smoke still rising from deeper ruins. He'd arrived at dawn to find catastrophe. He was leaving at midday carrying a burden he barely understood.

speaker: narrator
text: But the flame-path stretched before him, bright and insistent and impossible to ignore.

speaker: gareth
text: "Take this,"

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth said, pressing a small pouch into Caelin's hand. It clinked with coin.

speaker: caelin
text: "Not much, but enough to get you through the next few towns. And this -- "

speaker: narrator
text: He pulled a folded piece of cloth from his pocket, opened it to reveal a simple silver pendant on a leather cord.

speaker: gareth
text: "My wife's. She'd want you to have it. For luck."

speaker: caelin
text: "I can't -- "

speaker: gareth
text: "You can, and you will."

speaker: narrator
text: Gareth closed Caelin's fingers around it.

speaker: caelin
text: "Honor her memory by surviving whatever's ahead."

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin nodded, unable to speak past the tightness in his throat.

speaker: narrator
text: They gathered what little they needed -- dried meat, water skins, a spare cloak for Vex -- and walked to the eastern edge of the village where the flame-path led. The thread of fire stretched across the fields toward where the land began its climb into the foothills.

speaker: narrator
text: Only Caelin could see it, but he felt its pull like a physical thing now, stronger with each passing moment. The scale pulsed in rhythm with it, urging him forward.

speaker: vex
text: "So we just... walk toward the mountains?"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex asked.

speaker: caelin
text: "Following something only you can see?"

speaker: vex
text: "That's the plan."

speaker: caelin
text: "Terrible plan."

speaker: narrator
text: But she adjusted her pack and checked her daggers.

speaker: vex
text: "I've followed worse."

speaker: narrator
text: They set out together, leaving Thornwick's smoke behind. The flame-path stretched before them like a thread connecting present to uncertain future.

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin kept his marked arm exposed -- there was no point hiding it, and the scale seemed to pulse brighter when covered, as if objecting to concealment.

speaker: narrator
text: As they walked, the embedded scale kept time with his heartbeat, a steady rhythm that was both foreign and intimate. The filaments beneath his skin had settled into their patterns, no longer feeling like invasion but like something that had always been there, waiting.

speaker: narrator
text: The Emberpeaks grew larger on the horizon, their slopes darkening from blue to grey as afternoon light shifted. Somewhere in those mountains waited the First Sanctum. Somewhere ahead lay the Dragon's Ember, first of the Nine Relics.

speaker: narrator
text: And somewhere beyond that -- beyond the paths and purposes and ancient pacts -- waited the thing that must never wake.

speaker: narrator
text: But that was future's problem. Present's problem was putting one foot in front of the other, following a path of fire only he could see, trusting a scale embedded in his flesh to guide him true.

speaker: vex
text: "So,"

speaker: narrator
text: Vex said after they'd walked in silence for a while,

speaker: caelin
text: "what happens when we actually reach this sanctum? Do you have a plan?"

speaker: vex
text: "Find the relic. Don't die. Probably in that order."

speaker: caelin
text: "That's not a plan. That's barely even hope."

speaker: narrator
text: The scale pulsed warm. No sting. It was the truth.

speaker: caelin
text: "It's what I have,"

speaker: narrator
text: Caelin said.

speaker: vex
text: "Then I guess it'll have to be enough."

speaker: narrator
text: Vex squinted toward the mountains.

speaker: caelin
text: "Though for the record, I still can't see anything except regular mountains and impending doom."

speaker: vex
text: "The doom's definitely there."

speaker: caelin
text: "Fantastic."

speaker: narrator
text: But there was something almost like humor in her voice.

speaker: vex
text: "At least we're doomed together. That's practically a friendship."

speaker: narrator
text: Despite everything -- the weight of the mark, the magnitude of the task, the uncertainty of what lay ahead -- Caelin found himself almost smiling.

speaker: narrator
text: The flame-path stretched onward. The scale pulsed steady. The mountains waited.

speaker: narrator
text: And somewhere in the distance, something ancient stirred in its sleep, feeling the first link of the chain being forged anew.
